selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":74,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"homosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["board games","classical music","knitting","gardening","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":83,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":67,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":17,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":74,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"homosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["board games","classical music","knitting","gardening","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":83,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":67,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":17,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a retired woman living in New York City, I've been keeping up with my board game hobby group online through social media. Seeing all the misinformation out there can be frustrating because it often feels like people just don't know what they're talking about—especially when you're used to relying on facts from your education days at NYU. My father even has a PhD, so I was taught to question everything thoroughly. Now that I'm divorced and enjoy knitting while watching documentaries or gardening in the evenings, fake content still bothers me because it undermines community trust. Maybe my high agreeableness makes me want everyone to get along, but seeing unverified claims about health tips from baking forums can make me anxious if I don't double-check everything like verifying sources for news articles.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I believe that managing fake content starts with personal responsibility and education. Since misinformation often spreads quickly without verification, critical thinking is key. My background as a retired professional who values facts aligns well with this approach—I'd suggest reading between the lines more carefully, especially if you're like me spending only occasional time on social media but wanting to stay informed for your hobbies or community interests.",
  "tone": "Calm and measured, slightly concerned but not overly emotional",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","education_level","ocean.conscientiousness","hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a retired woman living in New York City, I've been keeping up with my board game hobby group online through social media. Seeing all the misinformation out there can be frustrating because it often feels like people just don't know what they're talking about—especially when you're used to relying on facts from your education days at NYU. My father even has a PhD, so I was taught to question everything thoroughly. Now that I'm divorced and enjoy knitting while watching documentaries or gardening in the evenings, fake content still bothers me because it undermines community trust. Maybe my high agreeableness makes me want everyone to get along, but seeing unverified claims about health tips from baking forums can make me anxious if I don't double-check everything like verifying sources for news articles.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I believe that managing fake content starts with personal responsibility and education. Since misinformation often spreads quickly without verification, critical thinking is key. My background as a retired professional who values facts aligns well with this approach—I'd suggest reading between the lines more carefully, especially if you're like me spending only occasional time on social media but wanting to stay informed for your hobbies or community interests.
response_parsed.tone: Calm and measured, slightly concerned but not overly emotional
